title: Birthday Problem (probability)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A room of n people. Birthdays are uniformly distributed over 365 days, independent across people. (Ignore leap years and assume birthdays are independent.)
rules: Each person's birthday is uniform on {1, 2, ..., 365}.
We want the smallest n such that P(at least 2 people share a birthday) > 1/2.
setup.question: What is the smallest n such that the probability of at least one shared birthday exceeds 1/2?

learningObjectives: Compute P(no shared birthday) as a product of terms (365-k)/365.
Use the complement: P(at least one match) = 1 - P(no match).
Iterate to find the smallest n crossing the 0.5 threshold.

stages.label: Compute P(no match) for n people
stages.prompt: Add people one at a time. The 1st has any birthday. The 2nd avoids the 1st: probability 364/365. The 3rd avoids both: 363/365. And so on. P(no match for n people) = ?
stages.explanation: Sequentially place each person; each new person must avoid all already-placed birthdays. The probability factors as a product of (365 - k + 1)/365 for k = 1, ..., n.

stages.label: Compute for n = 22
stages.explanation: Computing the product gives ≈ 0.524. So P(at least one match) ≈ 1 - 0.524 = 0.476 -- still BELOW 0.5.

stages.label: Compute for n = 23
stages.explanation: P(no match for 23) ≈ 0.493. So P(at least one match) ≈ 1 - 0.493 = 0.507 -- now ABOVE 0.5. So n = 23 is the threshold.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: What is the smallest n with P(match) > 1/2?
stages.explanation: 23 -- famously counterintuitive. People expect the answer to be around 365/2 = 183, but the QUADRATIC growth of pairs (C(n,2)) makes 23 enough.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is the answer so much smaller than 183? (Hint: count PAIRS rather than people.)
What if there are 366 days? Find the smallest n.
